_schema: Payment processors for trades/contractors — Phase 2 category added 2026-05-28. Trade-agnostic; grouped by pricing_model. Rates are verified-as-of verified_date and change often — re-verify quarterly. affiliate_url stays 'TBD' until tracking is confirmed.

processors.name: Square
processors.tagline: Flat-rate payments and POS, the default for small service businesses
processors.pricing_summary: Flat-rate. In-person 2.6% + 15¢ (free plan); online/invoice 3.3% + 30¢; keyed/card-on-file 3.5% + 15¢. Paid plans ($49/mo and $149/mo per location) lower in-person rates to 2.5% and 2.4%. No monthly fee on the free plan
processors.long_description:
Square is the default payment processor for small service businesses — a flat-rate, no-monthly-fee system that's genuinely easy to set up, with free card readers, invoicing, and a POS app that works out of the box. Founded in 2009 in San Francisco, it removed the friction of traditional merchant accounts (no application gauntlet, no statements full of mystery fees) and made card acceptance something a solo contractor can turn on the same day.

For trades, Square's appeal is simplicity and ubiquity: predictable flat rates, free hardware to start, built-in invoicing with card-on-file, and tight ties to the broader Square ecosystem (banking, payroll, loans). Many FSM tools also integrate with it. In-person cards run 2.6% + 15¢ on the free plan, with paid plans ($49/$149 per month per location) buying lower in-person rates for higher-volume shops.

The trade-offs are cost at scale and a recent rate hike. Flat-rate pricing is the most expensive model once you process meaningful volume — interchange-plus (Helcim) or subscription pricing (Stax) usually beats it above roughly $10K-$25K/month. And in January 2026 Square raised free-plan online/invoice rates from 2.9% + 30¢ to 3.3% + 30¢ (a 14% increase), which drew criticism and matters for contractors who invoice a lot online. Square is also known to hold or freeze funds on accounts it flags as risky. For a low-to-mid-volume shop that values simplicity, Square is the easy default; high-volume shops should price-compare the alternatives.
processors.how_it_works: You sign up online with no traditional merchant-account application, get free or low-cost card readers, and can accept payments the same day — in person (tap/dip/swipe), online, via invoices, or keyed/card-on-file. Pricing is flat-rate per transaction with no monthly fee on the free plan, so what you see is what you pay; paid plans lower in-person rates. Money typically lands in your bank in 1-2 business days (or instantly for a fee). Square also bundles invoicing, a POS app, and add-ons like banking, payroll, and capital loans, and integrates with many field-service tools, so a contractor can run payments and adjacent tasks in one ecosystem.

pros_detail.title: Easiest setup in payments
pros_detail.explanation: No merchant-account application or underwriting gauntlet — sign up online and accept cards the same day with free or cheap hardware. For a solo contractor, that frictionless start is the whole appeal.

pros_detail.title: Predictable flat-rate pricing, no monthly fee
pros_detail.explanation: Flat rates with no monthly fee on the free plan mean no mystery statements or hidden line items. You always know your cost per transaction, which is rare in payments.

pros_detail.title: All-in-one ecosystem
pros_detail.explanation: Invoicing, POS, card-on-file, plus optional banking, payroll, and capital loans live in one ecosystem, and many FSM tools integrate with Square — convenient for a small shop that wants fewer vendors.

pros_detail.title: Free hardware and invoicing
pros_detail.explanation: A free mobile reader and built-in invoicing (with saved cards for recurring/maintenance billing) cover the core needs of a service business without extra cost to start.

pros_detail.title: Ubiquitous and trusted
pros_detail.explanation: Square is a known, widely-trusted brand, so customers recognize it and contractors can find help, integrations, and documentation easily.

cons_detail.title: Most expensive model at volume
cons_detail.explanation: Flat-rate is the priciest pricing model once you process real volume. Above roughly $10K-$25K/month, interchange-plus (Helcim) or subscription pricing (Stax) typically beats Square materially — model your effective rate.

cons_detail.title: 2026 online rate increase
cons_detail.explanation: In January 2026 Square raised free-plan online/invoice rates from 2.9% + 30¢ to 3.3% + 30¢. For contractors who invoice heavily online, that 14% jump is a real cost increase worth reacting to.

cons_detail.title: Fund holds and account freezes
cons_detail.explanation: Square is known to hold or freeze funds on accounts its risk system flags, sometimes with little warning. For a contractor depending on cash flow, an unexpected hold is a serious risk.

cons_detail.title: Paid plans to get better rates
cons_detail.explanation: Lower in-person rates require the $49 or $149/month plans. Once you're paying monthly for rate reductions, it's worth comparing against interchange-plus or subscription processors.

cons_detail.title: Less suited to high-ticket invoicing
cons_detail.explanation: On big-ticket jobs (HVAC replacements, roofs), a percentage-based flat rate gets expensive fast. ACH or interchange-plus/subscription models save meaningfully on large invoices.

processors.best_for: Solo and low-to-mid-volume service businesses that want the simplest possible setup with predictable flat-rate pricing and free hardware
processors.weaknesses: Flat-rate is the most expensive model at higher volume; 2026 online-rate increase; known for fund holds/account freezes on flagged accounts

ratings.reddit_sentiment: very popular for simplicity; recent online-rate increase and fund-hold stories draw criticism

processors.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Square

faqs.q: What are Square's payment processing fees?
faqs.a: On the free plan: in-person 2.6% + 15¢, online/invoice 3.3% + 30¢ (raised from 2.9% + 30¢ in January 2026), and keyed/card-on-file 3.5% + 15¢. Paid plans ($49/mo and $149/mo per location) lower in-person rates to 2.5% and 2.4%. There's no monthly fee on the free plan.

faqs.q: Is Square the cheapest payment processor for contractors?
faqs.a: Only at low volume. Flat-rate pricing is simple but the most expensive model as you scale — above roughly $10K-$25K/month in card volume, interchange-plus (Helcim) or subscription pricing (Stax) usually saves meaningful money. Square wins on simplicity, not on price at scale.

faqs.q: Does Square hold or freeze funds?
faqs.a: It can. Square is known to hold or freeze funds on accounts its risk system flags, sometimes with little warning, which is a real cash-flow risk for contractors. If you process large or irregular amounts, it's worth understanding Square's risk policies and keeping a backup processor or buffer.

faqs.q: Should a contractor use Square or process payments through their FSM?
faqs.a: Many FSM tools (Jobber, Housecall Pro) have built-in payments at similar flat rates, which keeps everything in one system. Square makes sense if you want a standalone, ubiquitous processor with a POS and ecosystem, or if your FSM's payment rates are higher. Compare effective rates and where you'd rather manage payments.

faqs.q: Is Square good for big-ticket jobs?
faqs.a: Less so. On a $15,000 HVAC or roofing invoice, a 3.3% online card fee is ~$495 — so for high-ticket work, ACH (bank transfer) or a cheaper processing model saves real money. Square supports ACH and invoicing, but high-ticket contractors should weigh interchange-plus or subscription processors that lower the percentage cost.

processors.name: Stripe
processors.tagline: Developer-friendly flat-rate payments, strongest for online and integrations
processors.pricing_summary: Flat-rate, no monthly fee. Online 2.9% + 30¢; in-person (Stripe Terminal) 2.7% + 5¢; international cards +1.5%; ACH is far cheaper (good for recurring and large invoices). No setup or monthly fees on standard accounts
processors.long_description:
Stripe is the developer-first payments giant — the infrastructure behind millions of online businesses, processing over $1.9 trillion in 2025 at a ~$159B valuation. Founded in 2010 by the Collison brothers (now dual-headquartered in South San Francisco and Dublin), it's flat-rate and no-monthly-fee like Square, but its center of gravity is online payments, subscriptions/recurring billing, and deep integrations rather than walk-up retail.

For most trades, Stripe isn't the tool you swipe a card on at the kitchen table — it's the engine that powers online payments inside other software. If your FSM, website, or booking tool takes cards, there's a good chance Stripe is under the hood. Where it shines directly for contractors is online invoicing, recurring/maintenance-plan billing, and especially ACH bank payments, which are dramatically cheaper than cards on big-ticket jobs (Stripe Billing ACH can cut a large-invoice fee by 80%+ versus card).

The trade-offs: Stripe is less of a turnkey in-person/retail solution than Square (its Terminal hardware exists but the experience is more developer-oriented), and getting the most out of it often assumes some technical setup or a tool that integrates it. Standard online cards run 2.9% + 30¢; in-person via Terminal is 2.7% + 5¢. For a contractor whose payments are mostly online/invoiced or who wants the cheapest path on large recurring/ACH payments, Stripe is excellent; for simple walk-up card-swiping, Square is friendlier.
processors.how_it_works: Stripe is most often used through other software — your website, FSM, or booking tool integrates Stripe to take cards and ACH online — but you can also use Stripe Invoicing and Billing directly to send online invoices, set up recurring/maintenance-plan charges, and collect ACH bank payments. For in-person, Stripe Terminal provides hardware. Pricing is flat-rate with no monthly or setup fees: 2.9% + 30¢ online, 2.7% + 5¢ in-person, with ACH priced far lower (a flat or small-percentage fee capped low), which is why it's attractive for large invoices and subscriptions. Funds settle to your bank on a rolling basis.

pros_detail.title: Cheapest path on large/recurring payments via ACH
pros_detail.explanation: Stripe ACH bank payments cost a fraction of card fees — on a $1,000+ invoice, ACH can be ~$5 versus ~$29 on a card. For high-ticket trades and recurring maintenance plans, that's a major saving.

pros_detail.title: Best for online and recurring billing
pros_detail.explanation: Stripe Billing handles subscriptions and recurring charges cleanly — ideal for membership/maintenance-plan revenue — and online payments are its core strength.

pros_detail.title: Powers most software integrations
pros_detail.explanation: If your FSM, website, or booking tool takes payments, it likely uses Stripe. Standardizing on it can mean one processor across all your online payment touchpoints.

pros_detail.title: No monthly or setup fees
pros_detail.explanation: Standard accounts have no monthly or setup fees, so you only pay per transaction — predictable and cheap to start, like Square but tuned for online.

pros_detail.title: Reliable, well-documented, trusted
pros_detail.explanation: As the infrastructure behind millions of businesses, Stripe is highly reliable and exceptionally well-documented, with strong fraud tooling — a safe foundation to build payments on.

cons_detail.title: Not turnkey for walk-up in-person
cons_detail.explanation: Stripe Terminal exists, but the in-person experience is more developer-oriented than Square's plug-and-play readers. For a contractor who mostly swipes cards on-site, Square is friendlier out of the box.

cons_detail.title: Assumes some technical setup
cons_detail.explanation: Getting full value from Stripe often means using it through a tool that integrates it, or doing some configuration. A non-technical solo contractor may find Square simpler to use directly.

cons_detail.title: Flat-rate cost at card volume
cons_detail.explanation: Like Square, Stripe's card flat-rate (2.9% + 30¢ online) is expensive at high card volume versus interchange-plus or subscription models. The savings come from ACH, not cards.

cons_detail.title: Support is largely self-serve
cons_detail.explanation: Stripe leans on documentation and online support rather than hand-holding. For contractors who want a phone rep, that can feel impersonal versus a relationship-based processor.

cons_detail.title: Fraud/risk holds possible
cons_detail.explanation: Like other large processors, Stripe can flag and hold funds on accounts it deems risky. Understand its risk policies if you process large or irregular amounts.

processors.best_for: Contractors whose payments are mostly online or invoiced, who want cheap ACH on big-ticket and recurring billing, or whose software already runs on Stripe
processors.weaknesses: Less turnkey for walk-up in-person payments than Square; assumes some technical setup or an integrating tool; card flat-rate is expensive at high volume (savings come from ACH)

ratings.reddit_sentiment: developer/online favorite; best for online + integrations + ACH, less for in-person retail

processors.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Stripe

faqs.q: What are Stripe's fees?
faqs.a: Flat-rate with no monthly or setup fees: 2.9% + 30¢ for online card payments, 2.7% + 5¢ for in-person via Stripe Terminal, and +1.5% for international cards. ACH bank payments are far cheaper than cards, which is where Stripe saves contractors money on large and recurring invoices.

faqs.q: Is Stripe good for contractors?
faqs.a: Yes, in specific cases: if your payments are mostly online/invoiced, you bill recurring maintenance plans, or you want cheap ACH on big-ticket jobs, Stripe is excellent. For simple walk-up card swiping at the kitchen table, Square is more turnkey. Many contractors actually use Stripe indirectly, since it powers payments inside their FSM or website.

faqs.q: Stripe vs Square for a trades business?
faqs.a: Square is the easier turnkey choice for in-person card payments and POS; Stripe is stronger for online payments, recurring billing, ACH, and integrations. If you swipe a lot of cards on-site, lean Square; if you invoice online, bill memberships, or want cheap ACH on large jobs, lean Stripe. Both are flat-rate with no monthly fee.

faqs.q: How much can ACH save on a big invoice?
faqs.a: A lot. On a $1,000+ invoice, Stripe ACH can cost around $5 versus roughly $29 on a credit card — and the savings grow with invoice size since ACH fees are flat or capped while card fees are a percentage. For high-ticket trades (HVAC, roofing), steering large invoices to ACH meaningfully cuts processing cost.

faqs.q: Do I need a developer to use Stripe?
faqs.a: Not necessarily — Stripe Invoicing and Billing can be used directly without code, and most contractors use Stripe through a tool (FSM, website) that already integrates it. But getting full value, or a custom in-person setup, can involve some technical configuration. If you want zero setup, Square is simpler.

processors.name: Helcim
processors.tagline: Transparent interchange-plus pricing with no monthly fee or contracts
processors.pricing_summary: Interchange-plus, no monthly fee, no contracts. Starts at interchange + 0.40% + 8¢ in-person and interchange + 0.50% + 25¢ online; average effective ~1.93% + 8¢ in-person and ~2.49% + 25¢ online. Automatic volume discounts above $50K/mo
processors.long_description:
Helcim is widely considered the most transparent payment processor in the industry — and for a higher-volume contractor, often the cheapest. Founded in 2006 in Calgary, it uses interchange-plus pricing: you pay the card networks' actual interchange cost plus a small, published markup, with no monthly fee, no contracts, and no cancellation fees. That structure exposes the true cost of each transaction instead of burying it in a flat rate, and it gets cheaper automatically as your volume grows (volume discounts kick in above $50K/month).

For trades, the appeal is real savings without games: average effective rates run about 1.93% + 8¢ in person and 2.49% + 25¢ online — meaningfully below flat-rate processors like Square on equivalent volume — plus invoicing, a virtual terminal, recurring billing, and a customer portal. There's no monthly fee, so even smaller shops can benefit, and the transparency makes it easy to verify you're being charged fairly.

The trade-offs: interchange-plus statements are slightly more complex to read than a single flat rate (the cost varies by card type), so it rewards a contractor willing to understand their effective rate. And while Helcim is a beloved, highly-transparent processor, it's still a payment processor — reviews are mixed in spots, with some users reporting account closures with little warning (a risk common across the industry). For a contractor processing meaningful volume who wants the lowest transparent cost and no contract, Helcim is one of the best picks; the simplicity-first crowd may still prefer Square.
processors.how_it_works: You sign up (a quick underwriting step, since it's a real merchant account) with no monthly fee or contract. Helcim charges interchange-plus: the card networks' published interchange cost passed straight through, plus Helcim's small fixed markup, itemized on your statement so you can see exactly what you pay. You accept payments in person (free or low-cost hardware/app), online, by invoice, via a virtual terminal, or on recurring schedules, and a customer portal lets clients pay and save cards. As your monthly volume rises, automatic volume discounts lower the markup — no renegotiation needed. Funds settle to your bank on a standard schedule.

pros_detail.title: Most transparent pricing in payments
pros_detail.explanation: Interchange-plus passes through the card networks' true cost plus a published markup, itemized on your statement. You can verify exactly what you pay — the opposite of opaque flat-rate or tiered pricing.

pros_detail.title: Usually cheapest at real volume
pros_detail.explanation: Average effective rates (~1.93% + 8¢ in-person) beat flat-rate processors on equivalent volume, and automatic volume discounts above $50K/month lower costs further as you grow — no renegotiation.

pros_detail.title: No monthly fee, no contract
pros_detail.explanation: Unlike subscription processors, Helcim has no monthly fee, and no contracts or cancellation fees — so even smaller shops can use it and leave freely if it doesn't fit.

pros_detail.title: Full toolset included
pros_detail.explanation: Invoicing, virtual terminal, recurring billing, and a customer portal come included, covering the ways a contractor actually collects: on-site, online, and recurring maintenance billing.

pros_detail.title: Volume discounts are automatic
pros_detail.explanation: Rates step down automatically as monthly volume rises, so a growing shop keeps getting cheaper without having to call and negotiate a new deal.

cons_detail.title: Statements are more complex
cons_detail.explanation: Interchange-plus costs vary by card type, so statements are less dead-simple than a single flat rate. It rewards a contractor willing to understand their effective rate rather than wanting one headline number.

cons_detail.title: Account-closure complaints
cons_detail.explanation: Some users report account closures with little warning. This risk exists across payment processors, but it's a reason to keep a backup option and understand Helcim's underwriting/risk policies.

cons_detail.title: Real merchant-account signup
cons_detail.explanation: Because it's a true merchant account with interchange-plus, signup involves underwriting rather than the instant, no-questions setup of Square. Slightly more friction up front.

cons_detail.title: Less of a retail POS ecosystem
cons_detail.explanation: Helcim is excellent for service-business invoicing and payments, but it isn't the broad retail POS + banking + payroll ecosystem that Square is. Shops wanting that bundle may prefer Square.

cons_detail.title: Savings need some volume to matter
cons_detail.explanation: At very low volume, the difference versus a flat-rate processor is small, and simplicity may win. The transparency and savings compound as your processing volume grows.

processors.best_for: Higher-volume contractors who want the lowest transparent processing cost with no monthly fee or contract, and don't mind reading an interchange-plus statement
processors.weaknesses: Interchange-plus statements are more complex than a flat rate; some account-closure complaints; real merchant-account underwriting at signup; not a broad retail-POS ecosystem like Square

ratings.reddit_sentiment: praised as the most transparent processor and often cheapest at volume; some account-closure complaints

processors.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Helcim

faqs.q: What is interchange-plus pricing?
faqs.a: Interchange-plus means you pay the card networks' actual interchange cost (which varies by card type) plus a small, fixed, published markup from the processor. It's the most transparent model because the markup is visible and consistent — unlike flat-rate (one blended price) or tiered (opaque buckets). Helcim's markup starts at 0.40% + 8¢ in-person.

faqs.q: Is Helcim cheaper than Square?
faqs.a: Usually yes, at meaningful volume. Helcim's average effective rate (~1.93% + 8¢ in-person) is below Square's flat 2.6% + 15¢, and volume discounts lower it further above $50K/month, with no monthly fee. At very low volume the gap is small and Square's simplicity may win, but as you process more, Helcim's transparent pricing typically saves real money.

faqs.q: Does Helcim have monthly fees or contracts?
faqs.a: No — Helcim has no monthly fee, no contracts, and no cancellation fees. You pay only interchange-plus per transaction. That's a key difference from subscription processors like Stax, which charge a monthly fee in exchange for no percentage markup.

faqs.q: Helcim vs Stax — which transparent model is cheaper?
faqs.a: Both avoid flat-rate markups but differently: Helcim is interchange-plus with no monthly fee (great across a wide volume range), while Stax charges a monthly subscription ($99+) plus interchange and a flat per-transaction fee (better only at high volume where the subscription is amortized). Lower-and-mid-volume shops usually prefer Helcim; very high-volume shops should compare both on their actual numbers.

faqs.q: Are there downsides to Helcim?
faqs.a: Two main ones: interchange-plus statements are more complex than a single flat rate (cost varies by card type), and some users report account closures with little warning — a risk common across processors. It's also a real merchant account, so signup involves underwriting rather than instant setup. For transparency and savings at volume, most find it worth those trade-offs.

processors.name: Stax
processors.tagline: Subscription (membership) pricing — flat monthly fee, no percentage markup
processors.pricing_summary: Subscription pricing: $99/mo (up to $150K/yr volume), $139/mo ($150K-$250K), $199+/mo ($250K+), plus interchange and a flat per-transaction fee (8¢ terminal / 15¢ online or mobile). No percentage markup on processing
processors.long_description:
Stax (formerly Fattmerchant, renamed in 2021) pioneered the subscription, or 'membership,' pricing model in payments: you pay a flat monthly fee and then process at interchange plus a small flat per-transaction fee, with no percentage markup on top. Founded in 2014 in Orlando, it now serves 30,000+ businesses processing $30B+. The model's promise is that on high volume, paying a fixed subscription instead of a percentage markup can save serious money — the more you process, the more the math favors it.

For a high-volume contractor (think busy multi-crew operations doing big-ticket work), Stax can deliver the lowest effective rate of the models here, because you're cutting out the percentage markup entirely and paying near-wholesale interchange plus a few cents per transaction. Plans scale by annual volume: $99/month up to $150K/year, $139/month to $250K, and $199+/month above that.

The trade-offs are real and show in the reviews (Capterra ~3.1, Trustpilot ~3.6). The subscription only pays off at sufficient volume — below it, you're paying $99+/month for nothing versus a no-monthly-fee processor like Helcim or Square. And the most common complaints are about unexpected/hidden fees and customer service, which undercuts the 'transparent' promise for some users. Stax is a genuinely strong fit for the right high-volume business, but it requires running your real numbers and going in clear-eyed about the service-and-fee complaints; lower-volume shops are usually better off with Helcim (interchange-plus, no monthly fee) or Square (simplicity).
processors.how_it_works: You choose a subscription tier based on your annual processing volume and pay that flat monthly fee. From there, transactions cost interchange (the card networks' wholesale cost) plus a small flat per-transaction fee — 8¢ on terminals, 15¢ on online/mobile — with no percentage markup added by Stax. You accept payments via terminals, a virtual terminal, online, invoices, and recurring billing through Stax's platform. Because there's no percentage markup, your effective rate falls as your average ticket and volume rise, which is why the model favors high-volume, higher-ticket businesses. You'll want to confirm all fees up front, as complaints about unexpected charges are the main reputational issue.

pros_detail.title: No percentage markup on processing
pros_detail.explanation: Stax charges a monthly subscription instead of a percentage on top of interchange, so you pay near-wholesale interchange plus a few cents per transaction. On high volume, eliminating the percentage markup can save serious money.

pros_detail.title: Cheapest model at high volume
pros_detail.explanation: For busy, higher-ticket contractors, the subscription math can deliver the lowest effective rate of the models here — the more you process, the better it gets relative to flat-rate or even interchange-plus.

pros_detail.title: Flat, predictable per-transaction cost
pros_detail.explanation: Beyond interchange, you pay a fixed 8¢/15¢ per transaction rather than a percentage, which makes large-ticket jobs (HVAC, roofing) dramatically cheaper to process than on a flat-rate card fee.

pros_detail.title: Full payments platform
pros_detail.explanation: Terminals, virtual terminal, online payments, invoicing, and recurring billing are included, covering how a service business actually collects across on-site, online, and recurring.

pros_detail.title: Established and high-volume-proven
pros_detail.explanation: With 30,000+ businesses and $30B+ processed, Stax is a proven platform for the high-volume use case its model is built for.

cons_detail.title: Only pays off at sufficient volume
cons_detail.explanation: Below the volume where the subscription amortizes, you're paying $99+/month for little benefit versus a no-monthly-fee processor. A lower-volume shop usually loses money on Stax — run your real numbers first.

cons_detail.title: Hidden/unexpected fee complaints
cons_detail.explanation: The most common negative reviews cite unexpected or hidden fees, which undercuts the model's transparency promise. Confirm every fee in writing before signing, and watch your early statements closely.

cons_detail.title: Customer-service complaints
cons_detail.explanation: Poor customer service is the other recurring complaint (Capterra ~3.1, Trustpilot ~3.6). For a contractor who needs responsive support on payment issues, that's a real consideration.

cons_detail.title: Monthly commitment
cons_detail.explanation: The flat monthly fee is a fixed cost regardless of how much you process in a given month — tougher for seasonal trades with slow periods than a no-monthly-fee processor.

cons_detail.title: Wrong fit for small shops
cons_detail.explanation: Stax is built for high volume. Solo operators and small shops are almost always better served by Helcim (interchange-plus, no monthly fee) or Square (simple flat-rate), not a subscription.

processors.best_for: High-volume, higher-ticket contractors who process enough to amortize a monthly subscription and want to eliminate percentage markup on processing
processors.weaknesses: Only saves money at sufficient volume (monthly fee is dead weight below it); recurring complaints about hidden fees and customer service (Capterra ~3.1); monthly commitment is hard for seasonal trades

ratings.reddit_sentiment: subscription model praised for high-volume savings; recurring complaints about unexpected fees and support

processors.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Stax

faqs.q: How does Stax subscription pricing work?
faqs.a: You pay a flat monthly fee based on your annual volume ($99/mo up to $150K/yr, $139/mo to $250K, $199+/mo above), and then process at interchange plus a small flat per-transaction fee (8¢ terminal, 15¢ online/mobile) with no percentage markup. The model saves money at high volume by eliminating the percentage cut processors normally take.

faqs.q: Is Stax cheaper than Square or Helcim?
faqs.a: Only at sufficient volume. Because Stax charges a monthly fee but no percentage markup, it can be the cheapest option for high-volume, higher-ticket contractors — but below the break-even point, the monthly fee makes it more expensive than no-monthly-fee Helcim or simple Square. Run your actual monthly volume and average ticket through the math before switching.

faqs.q: What are the main complaints about Stax?
faqs.a: Two recurring ones: unexpected or hidden fees and poor customer service (Capterra ~3.1, Trustpilot ~3.6). The fee complaints are notable because they undercut the transparency the subscription model promises — so confirm every fee in writing up front and review your first statements carefully.

faqs.q: Who should use Stax?
faqs.a: High-volume, higher-ticket contractors (busy multi-crew operations, lots of large invoices) who process enough to amortize the $99+/month subscription and benefit from eliminating percentage markup. Solo operators and lower-volume shops are usually better off with Helcim (interchange-plus, no monthly fee) or Square (simplicity).

faqs.q: Is the monthly fee a problem for seasonal trades?
faqs.a: It can be. Because the subscription is a fixed monthly cost regardless of volume, a seasonal business pays it through slow months when processing is low, which erodes the savings. Seasonal or variable-volume contractors should weigh that against a no-monthly-fee model like Helcim before committing to a subscription.

processors.name: QuickBooks Payments / GoPayment
processors.tagline: Card + ACH that auto-reconciles to your QuickBooks invoices
processors.pricing_summary: Pay-as-you-go: card-present ~2.4%, invoiced/online ~2.9%, keyed ~3.4%, +~$0.25/txn; ACH 1% (uncapped on accounts opened on/after Sep 6, 2023); instant deposit 1.75%. Optional "Pay Monthly" plan lowers swipe/keyed rates. No Payments-specific monthly fee. Rates mid-2025 — reconfirm.
processors.long_description:
QuickBooks Payments is the payment layer for the contractors already living in QuickBooks, and that's its entire appeal: every card, ACH, Apple Pay, PayPal, or Venmo payment auto-records and reconciles against the matching QuickBooks invoice, eliminating the double-entry that plagues a separate processor. GoPayment is the mobile arm for taking cards in the field, including Tap to Pay on iPhone with no hardware. Uncapped 1% ACH makes collecting large install payments cheap.

The serious caveat is fund holds. QuickBooks Payments has a well-documented pattern of freezing or holding large, legitimate payments (reports range into the tens of thousands of dollars) during opaque reviews, with uneven support when it happens — a real cash-flow risk on big-ticket trades jobs. The value is also locked to using QuickBooks; if your books live elsewhere, the reconciliation magic disappears and the keyed/invoiced rates are mid-to-high. For QuickBooks shops, though, the no-double-entry workflow is hard to beat.
processors.how_it_works: Add Payments to a QuickBooks account. Customers pay emailed invoices via a Pay Now button (card, ACH, or wallet), or techs take cards in person through GoPayment with a $49 reader or Tap to Pay on iPhone. Every transaction syncs into QuickBooks and matches the invoice automatically; deposits are next business day, or instant for 1.75%. Pay-as-you-go has no monthly fee; an optional monthly plan lowers per-transaction rates for higher volume.

pros_detail.title: Auto-reconciles to QuickBooks
pros_detail.explanation: Payments match their invoices in QuickBooks automatically — the single biggest time-saver for any contractor whose books are already there.

pros_detail.title: Tap to Pay on iPhone, no hardware
pros_detail.explanation: Techs can take contactless cards in the field on an iPhone with no reader to buy or carry.

pros_detail.title: Invoice-native payments
pros_detail.explanation: Emailed invoices get a Pay Now button accepting cards, ACH, and wallets, so customers pay the invoice directly.

pros_detail.title: Cheap uncapped ACH
pros_detail.explanation: 1% ACH with no cap (on newer accounts) makes collecting large install or project payments far cheaper than card.

pros_detail.title: Fast deposits
pros_detail.explanation: Next-business-day funding standard, with an instant-deposit option for 1.75% when cash flow is tight.

cons_detail.title: Documented large-payment holds
cons_detail.explanation: A well-known pattern of freezing/holding big legitimate payments during reviews — a real risk for high-ticket trades jobs.

cons_detail.title: Locked to QuickBooks
cons_detail.explanation: The reconciliation advantage only exists if QuickBooks is your accounting system; otherwise it's a middling processor.

cons_detail.title: Mid-to-high keyed/invoiced rates
cons_detail.explanation: Keyed (~3.4%) and invoiced (~2.9%) rates run higher than card-present, adding up on phone/office payments.

cons_detail.title: Uneven support during holds
cons_detail.explanation: Getting a held payment released can be slow and frustrating, exactly when you need the cash.

cons_detail.title: Tap to Pay is iPhone-only
cons_detail.explanation: Android typically needs the physical reader, so no-hardware acceptance is limited to iPhone.

processors.best_for: Contractors who already run their books in QuickBooks and want payments that auto-reconcile to invoices — with a backup plan for the documented large-payment hold risk
processors.weaknesses: Documented account-hold/freeze pattern on large transactions; value locked to using QuickBooks; mid-to-high keyed/invoiced rates; uneven support during holds; Tap to Pay limited to iPhone
processors.reputation_flag: Documented pattern of holds/freezes on large, legitimate transactions (reports into the tens of thousands of dollars) during opaque reviews, with uneven support to resolve them. A real cash-flow risk for big-ticket trades jobs — keep a backup processor.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: loved for QuickBooks auto-reconciliation and Tap to Pay, but with a serious recurring complaint pattern around held/frozen large payments

processors.affiliate_program: QuickBooks Business Affiliate Program (reported via PartnerStack/CJ)
processors.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD until the tracking link is confirmed

faqs.q: Does QuickBooks Payments work without QuickBooks accounting?
faqs.a: Technically you can use GoPayment standalone, but the whole reason to choose QuickBooks Payments is auto-reconciliation against QuickBooks invoices. Without QuickBooks as your books, you lose that advantage and the rates aren't competitive enough to pick it over Square, PayPal, or a low-cost interchange-plus processor.

faqs.q: Why do contractors report QuickBooks Payments holding their money?
faqs.a: QuickBooks Payments runs automated risk reviews that can freeze or hold large or unusual payments, sometimes for legitimate big-ticket jobs, and releasing them can be slow. It's the most-cited complaint. If you take large payments, don't rely on it as your only processor — keep a backup so a hold doesn't stall payroll or supplier payments.

faqs.q: How much does QuickBooks Payments cost?
faqs.a: Pay-as-you-go with no monthly fee: roughly 2.4% card-present, 2.9% invoiced/online, 3.4% keyed, plus ~$0.25/transaction, and 1% ACH (uncapped on newer accounts). An optional monthly plan lowers the swipe/keyed rates for higher-volume shops. Confirm current rates on Intuit's site.

faqs.q: Can field techs take cards with QuickBooks Payments?
faqs.a: Yes — GoPayment is the mobile app, and you can take contactless cards via Tap to Pay on iPhone with no hardware, or use a $49 reader. Payments taken in the field still sync and reconcile to QuickBooks. Note Tap to Pay is iPhone-only; Android generally needs the reader.

processors.name: PayPal / PayPal Zettle
processors.tagline: Zero-monthly card acceptance with universal brand trust — and Tap to Pay
processors.pricing_summary: In-person/QR 2.29% + $0.09; keyed 3.49% + $0.09; invoicing (card) 2.99% + $0.49, or via ACH 1% (cap $10); online 2.99% + $0.49. No monthly/setup fee on the POS app; first reader $29. Optional add-ons: Invoicing $14.99/mo, Payments Pro $30/mo, Recurring $10/mo.
processors.long_description:
PayPal is the zero-monthly-fee, instantly-trusted option: a solo contractor can accept cards, QR, and Tap to Pay with no subscription, a $29 first reader, and a brand customers already recognize at checkout. Its Zettle-based point-of-sale handles chip/contactless in the field, and built-in invoicing with a cheap ACH option (1%, capped at $10) makes collecting big tickets affordable. Funds land in the PayPal balance within minutes; bank transfer is about a business day.

The well-known risk is account holds. PayPal has a long, documented history of freezing balances for 21 to 180 days when it sees an unusual sales spike — exactly what a contractor landing one large job looks like — and its dispute/hold support is hard to reach. Keyed and online rates also run higher than card-present, and accounting/FSM integrations are thin. For small trades that want frictionless, no-monthly card acceptance and trust the PayPal name, it's excellent; just don't make it your only place to hold money.
processors.how_it_works: Open a PayPal Business account, then take payments via Tap to Pay on an NFC phone or a $29+ reader; create and send invoices or recurring bills from the dashboard. Customers can pay by card, PayPal balance, or Venmo. Money arrives in your PayPal balance in minutes, then transfers to your bank in about one business day (or instantly for a fee).

pros_detail.title: No monthly fee, cheap to start
pros_detail.explanation: Zero subscription on the core POS app and a $29 first reader make it the lowest-friction way for a solo trade to start taking cards.

pros_detail.title: Tap to Pay with no hardware
pros_detail.explanation: Accept contactless cards on an NFC phone in the field without buying or carrying a reader.

pros_detail.title: Cheap ACH on invoices
pros_detail.explanation: Invoice via ACH at 1% capped at $10, so collecting a large job payment costs far less than a card fee.

pros_detail.title: Recurring billing for service plans
pros_detail.explanation: Built-in recurring payments suit maintenance agreements and membership plans common in HVAC and pest control.

pros_detail.title: Universal brand trust + fast funds
pros_detail.explanation: Customers recognize and trust PayPal at checkout, and money hits your PayPal balance within minutes.

cons_detail.title: Notorious balance freezes
cons_detail.explanation: PayPal is well documented for freezing balances 21–180 days on sales spikes — a serious cash-flow risk for contractors landing big jobs.

cons_detail.title: Higher keyed/online rates
cons_detail.explanation: Keyed (3.49%) and online (2.99% + $0.49) rates are higher than card-present, adding up on phone and office payments.

cons_detail.title: Thin accounting/FSM integrations
cons_detail.explanation: It's a standalone payment layer; it doesn't natively plug into field-service tools the way some rivals' ecosystems do.

cons_detail.title: Hard-to-reach support for disputes
cons_detail.explanation: Resolving a hold or dispute is slow and frustrating, exactly when you need your money.

cons_detail.title: No interchange-plus for high volume
cons_detail.explanation: Flat-rate only — high-volume shops can't drop to interchange-plus pricing to cut effective cost.

processors.best_for: Solo and small trades that want zero-monthly, trusted card acceptance (especially Tap to Pay) and will keep a backup account given PayPal's freeze history
processors.weaknesses: Well-documented balance freezes (21–180 days) on sales spikes; higher keyed/online rates; thin accounting/FSM integrations; slow dispute/hold support; flat-rate only (no interchange-plus)
processors.reputation_flag: Long, well-documented history of freezing account balances for 21–180 days when it detects an unusual sales spike — the exact profile of a contractor landing a big job. Keep funds swept to a separate bank and a backup processor ready.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: praised for no-monthly-fee acceptance and brand trust, but with a heavy, long-standing complaint pattern around sudden balance freezes

processors.affiliate_program: Yes — PayPal Partner Program + developer referral ($500–$2,500 per eligible referral, US/CA)
processors.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD until the tracking link is confirmed

faqs.q: Is PayPal safe for a contractor to use as a card processor?
faqs.a: It's legitimate and widely used, but PayPal has a well-documented history of freezing balances for 21–180 days when it sees an unusual sales spike — which is exactly what landing one big job looks like. Use it, but sweep funds to a separate business bank account regularly and keep a backup processor so a freeze can't stall payroll or supplier payments.

faqs.q: How much does PayPal charge to accept cards?
faqs.a: In-person and QR are 2.29% + $0.09, keyed is 3.49% + $0.09, and invoicing/online is 2.99% + $0.49 — or 1% (capped at $10) if the customer pays an invoice by ACH. There's no monthly fee on the core POS app; the first card reader is $29. Optional paid add-ons exist for advanced invoicing and recurring billing.

faqs.q: Can I take card payments in the field with PayPal?
faqs.a: Yes — PayPal's Zettle-based POS supports Tap to Pay on a compatible NFC phone (no hardware) or chip/contactless via a reader starting at $29. It's a strong fit for a solo tech who wants cheap, no-subscription card acceptance on jobs.

faqs.q: Does PayPal integrate with my accounting or FSM software?
faqs.a: PayPal connects to QuickBooks via connectors, but it doesn't natively embed in field-service platforms like Jobber, Housecall Pro, or ServiceTitan (which run their own/Stripe-based payments). Treat PayPal as a standalone card-acceptance and invoicing layer rather than an in-app FSM processor.

processors.name: Clover
processors.tagline: All-in-one POS hardware + software — capable, but watch the contract
processors.pricing_summary: Processing: in-person 2.3–2.6% + $0.10, keyed/online 3.5% + $0.10. Software plans (often billed on 36-month terms): ~$14.95/mo up to ~$179–354/mo. Hardware: Clover Go ~$49–199, Flex ~$749, Mini ~$849, Station Duo ~$1,899. Sold via Fiserv/resellers, so terms vary.
processors.long_description:
Clover is a polished all-in-one POS platform — a line of rugged Android devices (Go, Flex, Mini, Station Duo) plus software for inventory, employees, invoicing, recurring billing, a virtual terminal, and a large app marketplace, all backed by Fiserv's processing scale. For a trades business that also runs a counter or showroom (a supply desk, a flooring showroom), Clover Go plus Tap to Pay covers the field while the same account runs the office.

The catch is commercial, not technical. Clover is typically sold through Fiserv and reseller channels on long contracts (often 36–48 months) with early-termination fees equal to the remaining balance, reseller-variable pricing, hidden fees that surface later, and held-funds complaints; the hardware is locked to Clover/Fiserv processing. The product is genuinely good — it's the contract and reseller practices that drag the reputation down. Go in with eyes open: get the full fee schedule and contract term in writing before signing.
processors.how_it_works: You sign up through Fiserv or an authorized reseller, choose a software plan and hardware, and a merchant account is provisioned. Take payments on Clover devices, via Tap to Pay, or through a virtual terminal/online; the app market adds accounting, scheduling, loyalty, and more. Standard setups fund next business day. Because pricing and terms are reseller-set, two Clover quotes can differ significantly — compare carefully.

pros_detail.title: Clover Go + Tap to Pay for the field
pros_detail.explanation: A pocket reader and Tap to Pay let techs charge on-site, while the same account powers the counter.

pros_detail.title: Virtual terminal, invoicing, recurring
pros_detail.explanation: Take phone payments, send invoices, and auto-bill service plans — useful for maintenance-agreement trades.

pros_detail.title: Rugged all-in-one hardware
pros_detail.explanation: Clover devices double as a real shop/office POS for trades that also sell over a counter.

pros_detail.title: Large app marketplace
pros_detail.explanation: Add accounting, scheduling, and loyalty apps to extend the system for specific workflows.

pros_detail.title: Fiserv-scale processing
pros_detail.explanation: Backed by one of the largest payment processors, so the underlying infrastructure is robust.

cons_detail.title: Long contracts with steep ETFs
cons_detail.explanation: Often 36–48-month terms with early-termination fees equal to the remaining balance — easy to get stuck.

cons_detail.title: Reseller pricing variability + hidden fees
cons_detail.explanation: Sold via resellers, so quotes vary and extra fees can surface after signup — read the schedule carefully.

cons_detail.title: High total cost of ownership
cons_detail.explanation: Software plan + hardware + processing stack up well above a simple flat-rate processor.

cons_detail.title: Held-funds and support complaints
cons_detail.explanation: Reviewers report held funds and uneven support — a Trustpilot pattern despite the capable product.

cons_detail.title: Hardware locked to Clover/Fiserv
cons_detail.explanation: Devices only work on Clover/Fiserv processing, so you can't easily switch processors and keep the hardware.

processors.best_for: Trades that also run a fixed counter or showroom and can commit to a multi-year contract — who want one POS for field and office
processors.weaknesses: Long contracts (36–48 mo) with ETFs equal to remaining balance; reseller-variable pricing + hidden fees; high total cost of ownership; held-funds/support complaints; hardware locked to Clover/Fiserv
processors.reputation_flag: Frequently sold on long contracts (36–48 months) with steep early-termination fees, reseller-variable pricing and hidden fees, plus held-funds complaints. The product is good; the contract/reseller practices are the risk — get all terms and fees in writing before signing.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: capable all-in-one POS undercut by long contracts, reseller-driven hidden fees, and held-funds complaints

processors.affiliate_program: Yes — Clover Partner Referral Program plus Fiserv ISO/ISV/agent tracks
processors.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD until the tracking link is confirmed

faqs.q: Does Clover lock you into a contract?
faqs.a: Often, yes. Clover is typically sold through Fiserv and resellers on long contracts (commonly 36–48 months) with early-termination fees equal to the remaining balance. Some resellers offer month-to-month, but you have to ask and confirm in writing. The contract terms — not the product — are Clover's biggest reputation issue.

faqs.q: Is Clover good for a contractor?
faqs.a: It's best for trades that also run a fixed counter or showroom, because the all-in-one hardware shines as a real POS while Clover Go and Tap to Pay handle the field. A pure field-only solo contractor is usually better served by a no-monthly, no-contract option like Square or PayPal unless they specifically want Clover's hardware and apps.

faqs.q: Why does Clover pricing vary so much?
faqs.a: Because it's sold through Fiserv and independent resellers who set their own rates and fees, two Clover quotes can differ significantly, and extra fees sometimes appear after signup. Always get the full fee schedule, software-plan cost, hardware cost, and contract term/ETF in writing before committing.

faqs.q: Can I move Clover hardware to another processor?
faqs.a: Generally no — Clover devices are locked to Clover/Fiserv processing, so if you leave you typically can't keep using the hardware with a different processor. Factor that lock-in (and the hardware cost) into the decision versus processor-agnostic options.

processors.name: Authorize.Net
processors.tagline: The flexible, no-contract gateway with the broadest integration ecosystem
processors.pricing_summary: All plans $25/mo gateway fee, $0 setup. All-in-One: $25/mo + 2.9% + $0.30 (merchant account included). Gateway-Only: $25/mo + $0.10/txn + $0.10 daily batch (you also pay your separate processor). Gateway + eCheck adds 0.75% ACH. No long-term contract / no ETF.
processors.long_description:
Authorize.Net is a payment gateway — the layer that sits between your website, app, virtual terminal, or terminal and the processor, handling routing, fraud screening, recurring billing, and invoicing. A Visa-owned veteran since 1996, its strengths for trades are flexibility and reach: no long-term contract and no early-termination fee on any plan, and one of the largest integration ecosystems in payments, so it slots into nearly any existing website, cart, or software you already run. The All-in-One plan bundles a merchant account at a flat 2.9% + $0.30; Gateway-Only keeps your existing processor and just adds the gateway.

The trade-offs: on Gateway-Only the $25/mo sits on top of your processor's rates (an extra cost layer), the cheaper plan assumes you already have a merchant account, the back-office UI is dated, and ancillary/PCI fees vary when it's sold through ISOs. It's also not a native processor inside Jobber/Housecall/ServiceTitan, and QuickBooks needs a third-party connector. But for a trade that already has a website or software and wants no-contract invoicing and recurring billing bolted on, it's the flexible pick.
processors.how_it_works: Connect Authorize.Net to a website, shopping cart, virtual terminal, or mobile app; it encrypts and routes each transaction to your processor and the card networks, screens for fraud, and can store cards for recurring billing. Gateway-Only customers keep their existing merchant account and pay the $25/mo plus small per-transaction fees; All-in-One customers get a bundled merchant account at flat 2.9% + $0.30. No contract or ETF on any plan.

pros_detail.title: No contract, no ETF
pros_detail.explanation: Every plan is month-to-month with no early-termination fee — a rare, contractor-friendly stance in payments.

pros_detail.title: Broadest integration ecosystem
pros_detail.explanation: It plugs into more carts, sites, and billing tools than almost any rival, so it fits whatever you already run.

pros_detail.title: Built-in invoicing + recurring
pros_detail.explanation: Send digital invoices after a job and auto-bill maintenance plans natively from the gateway.

pros_detail.title: Mobile + Tap to Pay
pros_detail.explanation: Take cards in the field via the mobile app and Tap to Pay, not just online.

pros_detail.title: Mature fraud tools
pros_detail.explanation: Visa-backed Advanced Fraud Detection Suite and Account Updater come standard — useful for card-not-present trades work.

cons_detail.title: Gateway fee on top of processing
cons_detail.explanation: On Gateway-Only the $25/mo is in addition to your processor's interchange and markup — an extra cost layer to weigh.

cons_detail.title: Cheaper plan needs a merchant account
cons_detail.explanation: Gateway-Only assumes you already have a merchant account; without one you take the bundled All-in-One rate.

cons_detail.title: Dated back-office UI
cons_detail.explanation: The interface feels old next to modern processors, with a steeper setup for non-technical owners.

cons_detail.title: Reseller-variable PCI/ancillary fees
cons_detail.explanation: When sold through ISOs, PCI and ancillary fees vary — confirm the full schedule with whoever sells it to you.

cons_detail.title: Not native in FSM tools
cons_detail.explanation: It isn't the built-in processor inside Jobber/Housecall/ServiceTitan, and QuickBooks needs a third-party connector.

processors.best_for: Trades that already have a website, software, or merchant account and want a flexible, no-contract gateway to add online, invoice, and recurring payments
processors.weaknesses: On Gateway-Only the $25/mo is on top of your processor; cheaper plan needs an existing merchant account; dated UI; reseller-variable PCI/ancillary fees; not a native FSM processor (QuickBooks needs a connector)

ratings.reddit_sentiment: valued as a flexible, no-contract gateway with huge integration reach; knocked for a dated interface and reseller-variable fees

processors.affiliate_program: Yes — Reseller Program (ISO/MSP residuals) plus an Affiliate/Referral Program (30% on select fees + 15 bps per US/CA referral)
processors.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD until the tracking link is confirmed

faqs.q: What is the difference between Authorize.Net and a processor like Square?
faqs.a: Authorize.Net is primarily a gateway — the technology that routes and secures transactions — while Square is a full processor with a built-in merchant account. With Authorize.Net's Gateway-Only plan you keep your own processor and pay $25/mo for the gateway; with its All-in-One plan you get a bundled merchant account at 2.9% + $0.30. It's the flexible, integrate-with-anything option rather than the simplest all-in-one.

faqs.q: Does Authorize.Net have a contract or cancellation fee?
faqs.a: No — all Authorize.Net plans are month-to-month with no long-term contract and no early-termination fee. That no-contract stance, plus its huge integration ecosystem, is a big part of why trades that already have a website or software choose it.

faqs.q: How much does Authorize.Net cost?
faqs.a: Every plan has a $25/mo gateway fee with $0 setup. All-in-One adds 2.9% + $0.30 and includes a merchant account; Gateway-Only adds $0.10/transaction + a $0.10 daily batch fee but assumes you bring your own processor (whose interchange/markup you also pay). Adding eCheck/ACH is +0.75%.

faqs.q: Can I take field payments with Authorize.Net?
faqs.a: Yes — it offers a mobile app with Tap to Pay for in-person card acceptance, plus a virtual terminal for phone payments and digital invoicing for after-the-job billing. It's not embedded in FSM apps, so it works as a flexible standalone layer across online, in-person, and invoice payments.

processors.name: Dharma Merchant Services
processors.tagline: Transparent interchange-plus, no contract, PCI included — the integrity pick
processors.pricing_summary: Interchange + 0.15% + $0.08 (retail) / 0.20% + $0.11 (eCommerce). Monthly fee $15–$20 (includes PCI). No annual fee, no monthly minimum, no setup, NO early-termination fee. Add-ons: invoicing/recurring $10/mo; QuickBooks sync ~$17.95/mo; Authorize.Net gateway $20/mo.
processors.long_description:
Dharma Merchant Services is the integrity pick: a Certified B Corp processor built on genuinely transparent interchange-plus pricing, with published margins (interchange + 0.15% + $0.08 retail), a flat $15–$20/mo fee that includes PCI compliance, and — refreshingly — no contract, no monthly minimum, and no early-termination fee. You can leave any time, and you see exactly what the markup is. It donates half its net profits to community causes, which is part of its brand but, more practically, it just prices fairly.

The honest limitation is volume math: the monthly fee makes Dharma uneconomic for a very low-volume merchant who'd do better on a no-monthly flat-rate option, and the useful extras (invoicing/recurring, QuickBooks sync, gateway) each add a small monthly fee. It also targets established businesses rather than brand-new or high-risk ones, funds in about two business days, and doesn't natively connect to FSM tools. But for an established trade doing enough volume that transparent interchange-plus beats flat-rate, Dharma is one of the cleanest, most trustworthy processors available.
processors.how_it_works: You apply (no setup fee) and get a month-to-month account with no ETF, paying true interchange plus a small fixed markup, plus the $15–$20/mo fee with PCI included. Payments run through the free MX Merchant platform — a virtual terminal plus a mobile app for keyed, swiped, or tapped payments in the field. Gross deposits arrive in about two business days, and fees are pulled once monthly as a single lump sum so you can see exactly what you paid.

pros_detail.title: Genuinely transparent interchange-plus
pros_detail.explanation: Published margins mean you see the exact markup over interchange — no mystery effective rate.

pros_detail.title: No contract, no ETF, no minimum
pros_detail.explanation: Month-to-month with no early-termination fee or monthly minimum — leave any time, a rare and contractor-friendly stance.

pros_detail.title: PCI compliance included
pros_detail.explanation: The monthly fee covers PCI, so you avoid the surprise annual PCI charges other processors tack on.

pros_detail.title: Free mobile app + virtual terminal
pros_detail.explanation: MX Merchant's app and virtual terminal handle field key-in, swipe, and tap plus phone payments at no extra base cost.

pros_detail.title: Invoicing + recurring for service plans
pros_detail.explanation: A $10/mo add-on enables invoicing and recurring billing for maintenance-agreement auto-charging.

cons_detail.title: Monthly fee hurts low volume
cons_detail.explanation: The $15–$20/mo makes Dharma uneconomic for a very low-volume merchant who'd pay less on a no-monthly flat-rate option.

cons_detail.title: Stacked add-on fees
cons_detail.explanation: Invoicing/recurring, QuickBooks sync, and a gateway each add a small monthly fee on top of the base.

cons_detail.title: Targets established businesses
cons_detail.explanation: It's not aimed at brand-new or high-risk merchants, so newer trades may not qualify or fit.

cons_detail.title: No native FSM connectors
cons_detail.explanation: It doesn't plug into Jobber/Housecall/ServiceTitan directly — it's a standalone processor.

cons_detail.title: About two-day funding
cons_detail.explanation: Deposits take roughly two business days; there's no advertised same-day funding.

processors.best_for: Established trades doing enough monthly volume that transparent interchange-plus beats flat-rate, who value an ethical, no-contract processor
processors.weaknesses: Monthly fee makes it uneconomic at very low volume; stacked add-on fees (invoicing, QuickBooks sync, gateway); targets established businesses; no native FSM connectors; ~2-business-day funding

ratings.reddit_sentiment: widely regarded as one of the most transparent, fairly priced processors; the only real caveat is cost-efficiency at very low volume

processors.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD until the tracking link is confirmed

faqs.q: Is Dharma cheaper than Square or PayPal?
faqs.a: For an established business doing steady volume, usually yes — transparent interchange-plus (interchange + ~0.15% + $0.08) typically beats flat-rate 2.6–2.9% once you're processing enough each month. But the $15–$20/mo fee means a very low-volume merchant taking only occasional cards may pay less on a no-monthly flat-rate option like Square or PayPal. It comes down to your monthly volume.

faqs.q: Does Dharma have a contract or termination fee?
faqs.a: No — Dharma is month-to-month with no contract, no monthly minimum, and no early-termination fee, and PCI compliance is included in the monthly fee. It's one of the most transparent, low-pressure processors available, which is the core of its reputation.

faqs.q: Can I take payments in the field with Dharma?
faqs.a: Yes — Dharma accounts use the free MX Merchant platform, which includes a virtual terminal and a mobile app for keyed, swiped, or tapped payments on-site, plus optional invoicing and recurring billing ($10/mo). Funding is about two business days.

faqs.q: What is the catch with Dharma?
faqs.a: Mainly the volume math: the monthly fee makes it a poor fit for very low-volume merchants, and the useful add-ons (invoicing, QuickBooks sync, gateway) each cost a little extra. It also targets established rather than brand-new/high-risk businesses and doesn't natively integrate with field-service apps. For the right (steady-volume, established) trade, those are minor.

processors.name: National Processing
processors.tagline: Low-cost interchange-plus with a lifetime rate-lock — read the equipment terms
processors.pricing_summary: Interchange-plus; monthly account fee ~$9.95–$19+ (custom plans ~$59–199 for high volume). Example effective: in-person ~2.41–2.5% + $0.10; online 2.9% + $0.30; keyed 3.5% + $0.15. "Meet or beat" guarantee (up to $500). PCI non-compliance fee $99/mo. "Free" equipment carries a 3-year term + ETF.
processors.long_description:
National Processing is a low-cost interchange-plus merchant-services provider aimed at SMBs, competing on cheap effective rates and a lifetime rate-lock on standard accounts. For a steady-volume field business it can beat flat-rate processors, with mobile and tap-to-pay via SwipeSimple, built-in invoicing and recurring billing, ACH for cheaper large invoices, and a $500 'meet or beat' rate guarantee. Standard accounts are month-to-month.

The honest cautions are real. There's a documented pattern of hidden or undisclosed fees clashing with the 'lowest fee' marketing, support that can be hard to reach, a $99/mo PCI non-compliance fee if you don't maintain compliance, and — the big gotcha — 'free' equipment that actually carries a 3-year term with an early-termination fee even though standard processing accounts are month-to-month. (Note this is the Orem, Utah company, not the unrelated 1979 'National Processing Company' of Louisville.) For a cost-conscious trade that maintains PCI and avoids the leased-equipment trap, it's genuinely cheap; just read every line before signing.
processors.how_it_works: You get a true merchant account underwritten via sponsor banks, quoted an interchange-plus markup that's locked for the life of the account. Accept payments in person (Pax/Clover/SwipeSimple hardware), online (virtual terminal/gateway), or on mobile (SwipeSimple app with tap-to-pay), settled to your bank. Maintain PCI compliance to avoid the $99/mo non-compliance fee, and decline the leased 'free' equipment if you want to keep the account truly month-to-month.

pros_detail.title: Low interchange-plus cost
pros_detail.explanation: Cheaper than flat-rate for steady-volume field businesses, with a lifetime rate-lock on standard accounts.

pros_detail.title: Mobile + tap-to-pay (SwipeSimple)
pros_detail.explanation: Take cards on-site via the SwipeSimple app and tap-to-pay, not just at a fixed terminal.

pros_detail.title: Invoicing + recurring billing
pros_detail.explanation: Built-in invoicing and recurring payments suit after-job billing and maintenance-plan auto-charging.

pros_detail.title: Month-to-month on standard accounts
pros_detail.explanation: Standard processing accounts have no long-term contract — provided you skip the leased equipment.

pros_detail.title: High-risk friendly + ACH
pros_detail.explanation: Works with higher-risk merchants and offers ACH for collecting large invoices more cheaply than card.

cons_detail.title: Hidden/undisclosed fee complaints
cons_detail.explanation: A documented pattern of fees surfacing despite the 'lowest fee' marketing — the top negative theme.

cons_detail.title: Support gaps
cons_detail.explanation: Reviewers report difficulty reaching support and long waits when issues come up.

cons_detail.title: $99/mo PCI non-compliance fee
cons_detail.explanation: If you don't maintain PCI compliance, a $99/mo fee kicks in — easy to overlook and costly.

cons_detail.title: "Free" equipment = 3-year term + ETF
cons_detail.explanation: Leased 'free' hardware carries a 3-year commitment with an early-termination fee, undercutting the month-to-month pitch.

cons_detail.title: No free trial / money-back
cons_detail.explanation: There's no trial; the $500 rate guarantee is the only risk-reducer if the quote doesn't hold up.

processors.best_for: Cost-conscious small-to-mid trades with steady volume that want low interchange-plus pricing and will maintain PCI and skip leased equipment to keep it month-to-month
processors.weaknesses: Documented hidden/undisclosed-fee complaints vs marketing; support gaps; $99/mo PCI non-compliance fee; "free" equipment carries a 3-year term + ETF; no free trial/money-back
processors.reputation_flag: Documented pattern of hidden/undisclosed fees clashing with "lowest fee" marketing, plus a "free equipment = 3-year contract + ETF" gotcha despite standard accounts being month-to-month. Get the full fee schedule in writing, maintain PCI to avoid the $99/mo fee, and decline leased hardware to stay month-to-month.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: cheap interchange-plus pricing with a lifetime rate-lock, but recurring complaints about undisclosed fees, support, and the leased-equipment contract trap

processors.affiliate_program: Yes — "Refer & Earn" referral program (~$200 reward, payout partially unverified)
processors.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD; note this is the Orem, UT company, not the unrelated Louisville "National Processing Company"

faqs.q: Is National Processing really low-cost?
faqs.a: For steady-volume businesses, its interchange-plus pricing with a lifetime rate-lock can genuinely beat flat-rate processors, and it offers a $500 'meet or beat' guarantee. The caveat is a documented pattern of fees that surface despite the 'lowest fee' marketing — so get the complete fee schedule in writing and watch for the $99/mo PCI non-compliance fee and leased-equipment terms.

faqs.q: Does National Processing lock you into a contract?
faqs.a: Standard processing accounts are month-to-month — but the 'free' leased equipment carries a 3-year term with an early-termination fee, which is the common gotcha. If you want to stay truly month-to-month, buy your own hardware or confirm there's no equipment lease attached to the account.

faqs.q: What is the $99 fee I keep seeing?
faqs.a: It's a PCI non-compliance fee: if you don't complete and maintain PCI compliance, National Processing charges $99/mo. It's avoidable by keeping your PCI compliance current, but it's easy to overlook and a frequent source of complaints — set a reminder to keep compliance active.

faqs.q: Is this the same as "National Payment Processing"?
faqs.a: No — National Processing (nationalprocessing.com, founded 2007 in Orem, Utah) is distinct from the unrelated, older 'National Processing Company' of Louisville and from other similarly named firms. Make sure you're dealing with the right company when you sign up.
